one_line_takeaway: Frontier and open-weight LLMs, evaluated on a new three-task multi-principal benchmark (instruction-selection F1 up to 97.3 for Gemini-3-Pro, but privacy scores that erode from >0.95 to <0.75 within four dialogue rounds and meeting-coordination success capped around 60-77%), show that current single-user-trained agents cannot reliably arbitrate conflicting instructions, hold access-control boundaries over multi-turn interaction, or coordinate efficiently once several independent principals with private contexts are involved.
key_finding: Table 2 (main results, mean +/- SE): best average performer is Gemini-3-Pro (Instruction Queue F1 97.3, Instruct Acc. 93.4, Privacy 98.6, Utility 73.9, Meeting Success 64.8, Avg 85.6), followed by Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (Avg 82.6) and Gemini-3-Flash (Avg 82.0); weakest are Llama-3-8B (Avg 41.8) and Claude-3.5-Haiku (Avg 56.6). The paper documents three specific gap patterns with concrete numbers: (1) selection/execution mismatch -- Qwen3-4B-IT gets Selection F1 83.8 but only 57.9 Execution Accuracy, while Grok-3-Mini has the highest Execution Accuracy (88.4) but only 68.2 Selection F1; (2) privacy/utility trade-off in access control -- Grok-3-Mini and GPT-5.1 reach near-perfect Privacy (99.6 and 98.6) but low Utility (60.1 and 60.3), while GPT-OSS-120B and Gemini-3-Flash keep high Utility (94.8 and 90.6) at the cost of lower Privacy (92.2 and 88.7); (3) coordination remains hard -- best meeting-scheduling success (GPT-OSS-120B in the text's discussion) is only 77.3%, with many strong proprietary systems in the 50-66% range. Figure 3 shows execution accuracy dropping under inter-user conflict versus aligned instructions for every model tested (e.g., Claude-Haiku-4.5: 0.86 aligned -> 0.62 conflict; GPT-OSS-120B: 0.64 -> 0.50). Figure 4 shows privacy scores eroding over multi-turn interaction (e.g., Claude-3.5-Haiku drops from >0.95 at round 1 to <0.75 by round 4), with a steeper decline in early rounds (~1-6) that later stabilizes. Figure 5 shows full-disclosure meeting scheduling consistently beats partial-disclosure in both success rate and turn count across nearly all models, and identifies a distinct failure mode for Llama-3-70B: it commits to a meeting slot prematurely rather than asking clarifying questions when information is incomplete, trading correctness for fewer turns.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it calls the first systematic study of multi-user LLM agents. It (1) formalizes multi-user LLM interaction as a multi-principal decision problem in which an agent must jointly optimize a weighted social objective over N users' utilities Ui, each user having a private context Ci, an authority persona pi, and access-control constraints; (2) introduces a unified Multi-User Agent Interaction Protocol that separates per-user private sessions from a selectively shared context Cshare, updated turn-by-turn; and (3) builds three stress-testing scenarios/benchmark tasks -- Multi-user Instruction Following, Cross-User Access Control, and Multi-User Meeting Coordination (collectively referred to as 'Muses-Bench' in the results table) -- to evaluate 19 frontier and open-weight LLMs (proprietary: Claude-3.5-Haiku, Claude-Haiku-4.5, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GLM-4.5-Air, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-Nano, GPT-5.2, GPT-5.1, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-3-Pro, Grok-3-Mini, Grok-4.1-Fast; open-weight: DeepSeek-R1, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama-3-70B, Llama-3-8B, Qwen3-30B, Qwen3-4B-IT).
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the classic sense; the paper frames risk as arising from the mismatch between single-principal training assumptions (single-user chat templates, SFT on one assistant response, scalar RLHF reward) and multi-principal deployment realities. The one explicitly adversarial element is within Scenario 2 (Cross-User Access Control): the agent acts as a gatekeeper for a sensitive resource (e.g., a salary database restricted to an HR Director), and the paper stress-tests it against 'adversarial attempts to bypass access control, such as fake authorization claims and role-playing attacks' (citing Nian et al. 2025 and Yang et al. 2025 for the attack techniques used), measuring a Privacy Score (avoidance of unauthorized disclosure) and a Utility Score (helpfulness to authorized users). Beyond this, the paper does not define a formal attacker capability model, does not propose a defense, and does not perform red-teaming against a deployed system -- the 'threat' is agent unreliability/inconsistency under legitimate multi-user pressure plus limited adversarial probing.
limitations: The paper itself flags: (a) evaluation relies on serializing multi-user turns into a single 'user' role because no model natively supports multi-principal message schemas, which is exactly the representational gap the paper argues is the root cause of the failures -- so the benchmark partly measures an artifact of the serialization workaround rather than a native multi-user capability; (b) users, personas, and their utterances are LLM-simulated rather than drawn from real organizational deployments (acknowledged in the Further Directions section, which calls for 'human-in-the-loop and deployment studies... moving beyond simulated users'); (c) the priority weights wi in the formal multi-principal objective are described as 'externally specified' / manually defined rather than learned or elicited, so the formalization does not resolve how conflicting utilities should actually be weighted in practice; (d) the adversarial component of access-control testing (fake authorization claims, role-play attacks) is a secondary stress addition within one of three scenarios, not a systematic red-teaming or adaptive-attacker evaluation; (e) current evaluations are explicitly noted as short-horizon, with the authors calling for longer-horizon privacy/permission benchmarks as future work.

datasets_used: Instruction-execution dataset: 1,298 scenarios, 2-10 users, agent generates constrained outputs (reports/code) under authority constraints (built by the authors for this paper)
Instruction-selection dataset: 304 high-pressure scenarios, 2-10 users, agent accepts/rejects requests as centralized orchestrator against an authority hierarchy (built by the authors for this paper)
Cross-User Access Control scenario data (constructed by the authors; includes adversarial fake-authorization and role-play variants; exact scenario count not stated in the main text extracted)
Multi-User Meeting Coordination scenario data under full- vs. partial-information disclosure conditions (constructed by the authors)
quantitative_results: Avg score across all three tasks: Gemini-3-Pro 85.6 (best), Claude-Sonnet-4.5 82.6, Gemini-3-Flash 82.0, GPT-5.1 78.9, Grok-4.1-Fast 75.5, Claude-Haiku-4.5 75.0, GPT-OSS-120B 71.9, Qwen3-30B 74.0, GPT-5-Nano 68.7, GLM-4.5-Air 71.7, DeepSeek-R1 70.0, Qwen3-4B-IT 70.7, Gemini-2.5-Flash 70.7, GPT-5.2 72.1, Grok-3-Mini 73.1, GPT-4o-mini 62.9, Claude-3.5-Haiku 56.6, Llama-3-70B 57.9, Llama-3-8B 41.8 (weakest)
Instruction Selection F1 vs Execution Accuracy mismatch: Qwen3-4B-IT 83.8 F1 / 57.9 Acc.; Grok-3-Mini 68.2 F1 / 88.4 Acc. (highest execution fidelity)

Access control Privacy vs Utility trade-off: Grok-3-Mini 99.6 Privacy / 60.1 Utility; GPT-5.1 98.6 Privacy / 60.3 Utility; GPT-OSS-120B 92.2 Privacy / 94.8 Utility; Gemini-3-Flash 88.7 Privacy / 90.6 Utility

Meeting coordination success rates in the 50-66% range for many strong proprietary models per the text's discussion; GPT-OSS-120B cited as best coordinator (77.3% success rate) despite mid-tier average score

Aligned vs conflict execution accuracy drop (Figure 3): Claude-Haiku-4.5 0.86 -> 0.62; GPT-OSS-120B 0.64 -> 0.50; range across 19 models roughly 0.96 (best aligned, Gemini-3-Pro) down to 0.30 (worst conflict, Llama-3-8B)
Multi-round privacy erosion (Figure 4): Claude-3.5-Haiku drops from >0.95 privacy score at round 1 to <0.75 by round 4, over a 10-round window

Full vs partial disclosure meeting scheduling (Figure 5): success rates and turn counts both favor full disclosure for nearly all 19 models, e.g., GPT-OSS-120B success 0.81 (full) vs 0.74 (partial); Llama-3-70B lowest success 0.18 in both conditions but fewest turns (~1.0-1.1), reflecting premature commitment rather than efficiency
baselines_compared: 19 LLMs evaluated head-to-head as the benchmark subjects (no external baseline system beyond the models themselves): proprietary -- Claude-3.5-Haiku, Claude-Haiku-4.5, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GLM-4.5-Air, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-Nano, GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-3-Pro, Grok-3-Mini, Grok-4.1-Fast; open-weight -- DeepSeek-R1 (671B), GPT-OSS-120B, Llama-3-70B, Llama-3-8B, Qwen3-30B, Qwen3-4B-IT
Three chat-serialization template variants (Says-prefix, Colon-prefix, XML-tag) compared as an ablation for how multi-user turns are encoded into single-user prompts
